# git rebase

This command is design to integrate the changes from one branch into another.

Example, merges *main* into *feature*.


```
git checkout feature
git rebase main
```

the branch structure will look like this:
![](https://wac-cdn.atlassian.com/dam/jcr:3bafddf5-fd55-4320-9310-3d28f4fca3af/03%20Rebasing%20the%20feature%20branch%20into%20main.svg?cdnVersion=377)

This moves all the commit of *feature* branch onto the tip of *main* branch and return a *es parfait* linear branch history.
But it also had some disadvantages like reduce safety and traceability, rewriting project history may be a catastrophic for your collaboration workflow. And because it removes the merge commit so that we can't know when upstream changes were incorporate into your branch.

>[!Warning]
>You should follow [[The Golden Rule of Rebasing]]

#### Interactive Rebasing

```
git checkout feature
git rebase -i main
```

#### Workflow walkthrough

Firsly, create a seperate branch for your feature.

![](https://wac-cdn.atlassian.com/dam/jcr:88bfb19f-81ca-4202-b5d1-f2d8fa8778f2/06%20Developing%20a%20feature%20in%20a%20dedicated%20branch.svg?cdnVersion=377)

**Local Cleanup**

Performing an interative rebase regurlarly to make sure that you have a focused and meaningful commit history.

example for rebasing 3 latest commits:
```
git rebase -i HEAD~3
```

If you want to rewrite the entire feature branch into main then you can use:

```
git merge-rebase feature main
```

The only thing other developer see is your finished product. *voila*
>[!warning]
>This only work in your local and should only be use in private branch.

>[!tip]
>You can rebase 2 private branch together

>[!warning]
>You should not use rebase after making a [[pull request]]. Do a cleanup your code using interactive rebase before that.





# References


[[Atlassian Git Tutorial]]

---
Fetched title/text from Atlassian Git Tutorial (used as a reference only):
Title: Atlassian Git Tutorial — Rebase
Summary: Rebase is a Git command that integrates changes from one branch into another by moving or combining commits onto a new base. It creates a linear history by replaying commits on top of the target branch. Because rebase rewrites history, it should generally be used on local/private branches, not shared branches. Interactive rebase (git rebase -i) is used to edit, squash, reorder, or remove commits during cleanup.
Key points:
- Use `git checkout feature` then `git rebase main` to move feature commits onto main.
- Rebase makes history linear, but can reduce safety/traceability because it rewrites commit history.
- Recommended for local cleanup before opening a pull request.
- Interactive rebase examples include `git rebase -i main` and `git rebase -i HEAD~3`.
- Rebase should not be used on published/shared branches unless you understand the impact.

# git rebase

This command is design to integrate the changes from one branch into another.

Example, merges *main* into *feature*.


```
git checkout feature
git rebase main
```

the branch structure will look like this:
![](https://wac-cdn.atlassian.com/dam/jcr:3bafddf5-fd55-4320-9310-3d28f4fca3af/03%20Rebasing%20the%20feature%20branch%20into%20main.svg?cdnVersion=377)

This moves all the commit of *feature* branch onto the tip of *main* branch and return a *es parfait* linear branch history.
But it also had some disadvantages like reduce safety and traceability, rewriting project history may be a catastrophic for your collaboration workflow. And because it removes the merge commit so that we can'\''t know when upstream changes were incorporate into your branch.

>[!Warning]
>You should follow [[The Golden Rule of Rebasing]]

#### Interactive Rebasing

```
git checkout feature
git rebase -i main
```

#### Workflow walkthrough

Firsly, create a seperate branch for your feature.

![](https://wac-cdn.atlassian.com/dam/jcr:88bfb19f-81ca-4202-b5d1-f2d8fa8778f2/06%20Developing%20a%20feature%20in%20a%20dedicated%20branch.svg?cdnVersion=377)

**Local Cleanup**

Performing an interative rebase regurlarly to make sure that you have a focused and meaningful commit history.

example for rebasing 3 latest commits:
```
git rebase -i HEAD~3
```

If you want to rewrite the entire feature branch into main then you can use:

```
git merge-rebase feature main
```

The only thing other developer see is your finished product. *voila*
>[!warning]
>This only work in your local and should only be use in private branch.

>[!tip]
>You can rebase 2 private branch together

>[!warning]
>You should not use rebase after making a [[pull request]]. Do a cleanup your code using interactive rebase before that.
